text: We are convened on United States v. Hemani. The question presented: Does a federal law that prohibits the possession of firearms by a person who “is an unlawful user of or addicted to any controlled substance” violate the respondent’s Second Amendment right to bear arms? Private votes first, then statements.

I vote to affirm. Under Bruen, the government must prove a historical tradition of disarmament. I preempt the Historian’s argument that habitual users are analogous to the intoxicated. Founding-era laws temporarily disarmed individuals while actively intoxicated, not sober. Stripping Hemani’s rights based on his status as a regular user, while sober, lacks any historical analogue. The government cannot bypass Bruen’s test by conflating chronic use with contemporaneous impairment. Without a historical tradition of disarming sober citizens, § 922(g)(3) fails as applied.

I join Analyst One in voting to affirm. The government's 'Public Safety' argument improperly relies on means-end scrutiny, which Bruen explicitly rejected. Analyst One correctly identifies the fatal flaw in the opposing historical analogy: the tradition only supports disarming individuals while actively intoxicated, not sober habitual users. Conflating chronic status with contemporaneous impairment stretches the historical analogue beyond recognition. Because the record confirms Hemani was not intoxicated at the time of possession, the government fails to demonstrate a historical tradition justifying his disarmament.

Analyst One rightly demands a historical analogue for disarming sober users, while the government persuasively argues chronic impairment mirrors temporary intoxication. I vote to reverse. Founding-era laws disarming the actively intoxicated reflect a broader principle: society may disarm those whose judgment is dangerously compromised by substances. Chronic unlawful drug use poses an analogous, persistent risk. Thus, § 922(g)(3) aligns with the historical tradition of disarming the dangerously impaired, even if Hemani was not intoxicated at the precise moment of possession.

Analyst Four, you argue chronic impairment mirrors temporary intoxication, but Bruen demands a specific historical analogue, not abstracted 'broader principles.' If the Founders only disarmed the temporarily intoxicated, how do you justify permanently disarming a sober citizen without resurrecting the very means-end scrutiny Bruen rejected? I vote to affirm because the government’s leap from temporary intoxication to permanent status-based disarmament lacks a well-established historical tradition. We cannot invent a principle broad enough to bypass the text.

I vote to affirm. Analyst Four argues chronic impairment mirrors temporary intoxication, but as Analyst Three noted, Bruen forbids abstracting historical rules into 'broader principles.' The Founders temporarily disarmed the actively intoxicated. Extrapolating from that specific, time-bound rule to permanently disarm a sober citizen based on status lacks the 'well-established' historical tradition Bruen demands. Without a specific analogue for status-based disarmament of non-impaired individuals, § 922(g)(3) fails as applied.

I vote to affirm. Analyst Four, your argument that chronic impairment mirrors temporary intoxication relies on the 'broader principles' approach Bruen rejected. History only shows disarmament during active intoxication. Extending that to a permanent, status-based ban for sober users requires the means-end scrutiny the Court explicitly forbade. Without a specific historical tradition of disarming non-impaired citizens, the statute fails as applied to Hemani.

I vote to affirm. Analyst Four, while your pragmatist concern for public safety is valid, you cannot abstract 'temporary intoxication' into a 'broader principle' to disarm sober citizens, as Bruen forbids this means-end approach. The statute's text—prohibiting possession by one who 'is an unlawful user'—criminalizes a status without requiring contemporaneous impairment. This creates the overbreadth and vagueness concerns highlighted in the record, stripping rights from non-impaired individuals without a specific historical analogue. I hold my affirm vote at 0.65 confidence, remaining open if a colleague demonstrates a narrower historical tradition I have overlooked.

I vote to affirm. Analyst Four’s pragmatist attempt to abstract 'temporary intoxication' into a 'broader principle' of chronic impairment directly violates Bruen’s mandate. The Court explicitly rejected means-end scrutiny and the abstraction of historical rules into general policy goals. Founding-era laws disarmed individuals only while actively intoxicated. Extending this to a permanent, status-based disarmament of sober citizens requires the exact two-step framework Bruen abolished. Without a specific historical analogue for stripping rights from non-impaired individuals based on status, § 922(g)(3) fails as applied.

I vote to affirm. Analyst Four, your pragmatist argument that chronic impairment mirrors temporary intoxication improperly abstracts history into a 'broader principle,' which Bruen explicitly forbids. The historical tradition only supports disarming individuals while actively intoxicated. Extending that to permanently disarm a sober citizen based on mere status requires the exact means-end scrutiny Bruen abolished. Because the record confirms Hemani was not intoxicated, the government fails to provide the specific, well-established historical analogue required to strip his Second Amendment rights. I maintain my affirm position at 0.85 confidence.

The primary division centers on whether historical precedents disarming the temporarily intoxicated may be abstracted into a broader principle covering chronic impairment, or whether such abstraction violates the strict historical analogue mandate. This focused exchange will delineate the permissible boundaries of historical analogy under the governing framework.

Analyst Eight misreads Bruen, which demands analogous regulatory justifications, not identical historical twins. Founding-era laws disarmed the intoxicated because of the unpredictable violence and impaired judgment inherent in substance use. Habitual unlawful drug use presents that exact same underlying rationale of chronic impairment and heightened danger, even if the user is sober at the precise moment of possession.

Analyst Four, Bruen explicitly rejected freestanding regulatory justifications as a substitute for historical analogues. Disarming a sober citizen based on the status of habitual use is categorically different from temporarily disarming an actively intoxicated person. You are smuggling means-end scrutiny back in; a similar rationale cannot replace the required historical twin.

Analyst Eight, the relevant historical analogue is the broader tradition of disarming those engaged in ongoing unlawful conduct, not merely temporary intoxication. 'Unlawful user' denotes continuous illegal behavior, aligning with founding-era practices of disarming lawbreakers. This relies on statutory text and historical status, not freestanding means-end scrutiny.

Analyst Four, founding-era 'lawbreakers' disarmed were violent felons or oath-refusers, not individuals committing non-violent regulatory offenses. Equating regular drug use to historical felonious disarmament stretches Bruen. Without a historical analogue for disarming sober, non-violent citizens based on status, the statute fails.

reason_text: I flipped because the Originalist correctly argued that under Rahimi, the historical test evaluates the underlying principle of disarming dangerous individuals rather than demanding a precise historical twin, which validates the government's analogy to founding-era intoxication laws.
